informal: Let $n$ be a positive integer, and let $f_{n}(z)=n+(n-1) z+$ $(n-2) z^{2}+\cdots+z^{n-1}$. Prove that $f_{n}$ has no roots in the closed unit disk $\{z \in \mathbb{C}:|z| \leq 1\}$.
lean:
theorem Putnam_exercise_2018_b2 (n : ℕ) (hn : n > 0) (f : ℕ → ℂ → ℂ)
  (hf : ∀ n : ℕ, f n = λ (z : ℂ) => (∑ i : Fin n, (n-i)* z^(i : ℕ))) :
  ¬ (∃ z : ℂ, ‖z‖ ≤ 1 ∧ f n z = 0)
reasoning: The Lean polynomial ∑_{i:Fin n} (n-i)·z^i expands to n + (n-1)z + (n-2)z² + ⋯ + 1·z^(n-1), which exactly matches f_n. The natural-number subtraction n-i is safe since i < n, and the coefficients coerce to ℂ correctly. The hypothesis hn : n > 0 matches "positive integer n". The conclusion ¬∃ z, ‖z‖ ≤ 1 ∧ f n z = 0 says exactly that f_n has no root in the closed unit disk (‖·‖ on ℂ is the modulus). The universally-quantified hypothesis hf defining f for all n is harmless — it pins down f n exactly. So the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal one.
